data.title: Pou Sheng International Logs 4% Drop in December 2024 Operating Revenue
data.description:
Pou Sheng International recorded an operating revenue of around 1.51 billion yuan in December 2024, down 3.9% from 1.58 billion yuan a year prior, a Friday bourse filing said.

For the year 2024, the sportswear retailer's operating revenue fell by 8% to 18.5 billion yuan from 20.1 billion yuan in 2023.

data.title: European Midday Briefing: Investors Await U.S. Jobs Data
data.description:
MARKET WRAPS

Stocks:

European indices were mixed ahead of the U.S. nonfarm payrolls data for December, due at 1330 GMT.

Rising Treasury yields have ratcheted up tensions for equity investors. The jobs report could determine whether they continue to climb or pull back.

U.S. Markets:

Stock futures were falling while bond yields surged and investors braced for the key jobs report.

Earlier this week, stocks and bonds sold off after better-than-expected economic data, with the JOLTS job-openings survey indicating the labor market stabilized after a summer slowdown.

And while economists forecast the unemployment rate held at 4.2% in December, it might be that the jobs market is weaker than it appears.

The number of unemployed Americans searching for work for at least six months has increased by more than 50% since the end of 2022.

Earnings are expected from Delta Air Lines, Constellation Brands, Walgreens Boots Alliance, WD-40, and Tilray Brands.

Stocks to Watch

Allstate, Travelers, and Chubb likely will be the publicly traded insurers most exposed to losses from the L.A. fires, according to J.P. Morgan. Allstate was down 5.1%, Travelers fell 4%, and Chubb declined 3.6%.

Nvidia was down 1.1% following a Bloomberg report that said the Biden administration plans to impose a final round of chip export restrictions before leaving office. Fellow chip makers also traded lower.

Forex:

The dollar is unlikely to move materially unless the payrolls report is much stronger or weaker than expected, MUFG Bank said.

Given the clear rhetoric from Fed officials about slowing interest rate cuts and with Trump's January 20 presidential inauguration approaching, it would likely take a notable miss versus expectations to see the dollar move significantly.

"We remain dollar-bullish, but moves could be more contained until we get confirmation of trade tariff announcements on or soon after inauguration day."

ING said the balance of risks are tilted toward dollar gains as possibly robust jobs figures could "prompt markets to price out a March rate cut and potentially push the first fully-priced move beyond June."

In the event of weaker-than-expected data, investors could trim dollar long positions that bet on its rise, however, these longs could be rebuilt at better levels ahead of key upcoming data and Donald Trump's January 20 presidential inauguration.

Sterling trimmed losses sustained Thursday following a sharp rise in U.K. government bond yields after a U.K. Treasury spokesperson said meeting the fiscal rules is non-negotiable.

"The market seems to be acknowledging the Treasury's reiterated fiscal pledges and this has prevented the gilt and pound selloffs from becoming disorderly," ING said.

UBS Global Wealth Management sterling could remain under pressure in the near-term on concerns about higher U.K. borrowing costs, but is likely to recover later in the year.

"A likely more cautious rate-cutting stance from the Bank of England versus its peers--as well as an eventual softening of the dollar's overvaluation--still supports a sterling revival to $1.34 by year-end."

With volatility and a weaker sterling set to persist in the near-term, investors could find value in structured strategies that generate income or gradually accumulate sterling until fiscal policy uncertainty eases and investor confidence rebounds, it added.

Bonds:

Bund yields are likely to find support soon, according to Citi.

"The selloff into [2024] year-end likely reflects a reassessment of how far the European Central Bank can cut if the Fed is anticipating just two 25bp cuts in 2025," it said.

"Given the space already made for supply via higher outright yields, we suspect the bulk of the selloff may be over," it added, however the global picture needs to be considered, starting with the imminent payrolls release for December.

A momentum led selloff in the 10-year Treasury toward 5% is a buying opportunity, Societe Generale said.

The 10-year Treasury yield is rising along with term premia and terminal fed funds rate expectations and declining equity risk premiums and a tightening of financial conditions are likely catalysts for a reversal, it added.

Gilt yields rose, although they stayed below multi-year highs reached Thursday.

Investors will be watching to see whether gilt-market turbulence resumes and the jobs data have potential to cause volatility, Tickmill Group said, adding that the key question would be whether the BOE opts to intervene to stabilize gilts if it sees signs of "disorderly markets."

Capital Economics said gilts have fared worse than others within a recent global government bond selloff, but they could recover soon.

"We think that bonds will recover before long, with yields in the U.K. falling particularly sharply by the end of this year."

Energy:

Oil rose and was on track for modest weekly gains, as colder weather in parts of the U.S. and Europe boosted expectations of stronger heating fuel demand.

Prices are also supported by uncertainties over the impact of future U.S. policy on energy markets, from potential fresh tariffs to stricter sanctions.

"Asian buyers have already been looking for alternative grades from the Middle East, with broader sanctions against Russia and Iran making this oil flow more difficult," ING said.

Earlier this week, Citi raised its first-quarter estimates for Brent to $71 a barrel and for WTI to $67 a barrel, citing colder temperatures and potential disruptions to Iranian and Russian oil exports.

Metals:

Gold was headed for a weekly gain of roughly 2% as uncertainty around future U.S. policy under Trump boosts safe-haven demand and traders awaited the U.S. jobs report for more clues on the path of interest rates.

According to Swissquote Bank, activity on Fed funds futures suggests the next cut would be in May.

But "a set of stronger-than-expected [jobs] data could flip this expectation to the 'no cut until June' side very rapidly, enhance the selloff in the U.S. treasuries and support a further appreciation of the U.S. dollar."

Gold Chart

Gold bulls are growing stronger after Comex gold futures closed above the 50-day simple moving average line on Thursday, RHB Retail Research said.

However, the technical setup is still bearish, given resistance at the $2,700 level is holding firm, and immediate support is pegged at $2,600.

EMEA HEADLINES

Mercedes-Benz Car Sales Fell 3% in 2024 Amid Challenging Market

Mercedes-Benz car sales fell 3% to 1.98 million vehicles in 2024 amid a challenging global market, with top-end vehicle sales weighed by market conditions in China, model changes and weak electric-vehicle demand.

European automakers have faced intense competition with local manufacturers in China in addition to lackluster demand for electric vehicles, while an economic slowdown and property market slump have also seen Chinese buyers shy away from buying high-end cars.

Sainsbury's Christmas Sales Grew on Higher Volumes, Expects Profit in Line With Market Views

J Sainsbury reported sales volume growth for the key Christmas period and said it expects to deliver retail operating profit for its fiscal year in line with market consensus.

The British grocer on Friday said sales excluding fuel rose 2.7% in the 16 weeks ended Jan. 4 compared with the same period a year earlier. Like-for-like sales rose 2.8%.

Vodafone Sells Remaining 3% Share in Indus Towers; Increases Stake in India's Vodafone Idea

Vodafone Group sold its remaining 3% stake in telecom tower company Indus Towers and used the 28.0 billion Indian Rupees ($325.9 million) raised in proceeds to repay debt and increase its stake in India's Vodafone Idea.

The U.K. telecommunication group said Friday that it has completed the placing of its 79.2 million shares in Indus Towers via an accelerated bookbuild offering, as previously announced in December.

French Industry Shows Glimmer of Recovery

French factory production recovered a little toward the end of last year in a glimpse of optimism for the beleaguered sector.

Manufacturing output rose 0.2% on month in November, figures from French statistics agency Insee showed Friday. That came against expectations for a third-straight month of decline in production, according to economists surveyed by The Wall Street Journal.

GLOBAL NEWS

Rival Republican Plans Compete for Trump's Approval

WASHINGTON-With full GOP control of the government just 10 days away, Republicans are trying to rough out the architecture of their "one big, beautiful bill" to cut taxes, reduce spending and boost border security.

Groups of House Republicans with conflicting priorities are headed to President-elect Donald Trump's Mar-a-Lago estate this weekend to lobby him and strategize, as leaders attempt to unite their party's factions and keep them together through months of fiscal negotiations. While the party controls both chambers of Congress, leaders have little margin for error in the House, where their 219-215 advantage will shrink to 217-215 after two members join the new administration.

China's Central Bank Suspends Bond Buying as Yields, Yuan Fall

China's central bank has hit pause on Chinese government bond purchases, a move that comes as long-term yields hit fresh lows amid expectations of more monetary policy easing.

The People's Bank of China cited excess demand for government bonds as the reason for its decision, saying the suspension was made effective this month. Buying will resume at an appropriate time, it said in a statement Friday.

Arab States Race Turkey for Influence in New Syria

Saudi Arabia and other Arab states are jockeying for influence with Syria's Islamist government, hoping to gain an advantage on rivals in the strategically positioned country despite misgivings about the jihadist past of its new leaders.

The kingdom, along with Jordan and Qatar, is rushing humanitarian aid and energy assistance to Syria's war-weary population. The Arab states are betting that doing so could advance both narrow and strategic goals-from cutting the flow of drugs and radical fighters across Syria's borders, to countering the influence of competitors such as Turkey and Iran.

Supreme Court Won't Block Trump's Sentencing in Hush-Money Case

WASHINGTON-The Supreme Court refused to block President-elect Donald Trump's criminal sentencing for covering up hush money paid to adult-film star Stormy Daniels, clearing the way for an unprecedented court proceeding Friday in New York that will brand the nation's returning leader as a felon.

By a 5-4 vote, the court rejected Trump's last-ditch arguments to avoid what his lawyers acknowledged was the "public stigma" felons carry.

Write to clare.kinloch@wsj.com

Write to us at newsletters@dowjones.com

We offer an enhanced version of this briefing that is optimized for viewing on mobile devices and sent directly to your email inbox. If you would like to sign up, please go to https://newsplus.wsj.com/subscriptions.

This article is a text version of a Wall Street Journal newsletter published earlier today.

data.title: BOE Should Keep a Cool Head Vis-a-Vis Gilt Selloff — Market Talk
data.description: The Bank of England is likely to take a cautious approach to the recent sharp selloff in gilts, says Ken Egan, director of European sovereign credit at rating agency KBRA. The central bank will avoid action that could draw unnecessary attention to financial stability concerns--which could be somewhat overstated at the moment--, the says. "Amid this market volatility, it's worth noting that demand for U.K. debt remains notably strong." Expectations for BOE rate cuts have been scaled back lately, reflecting a reassessment of expectations for inflation which is now predicted to be higher. This aligns with a broader resurgence of a 'higher for longer' narrative regarding interest rates globally, he says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Sunshine Guojian Pharmaceutical Gets China Clinical Trial Nod for Autoimmune Condition Injection
data.description:
China's medical products administrator approved Sunshine Guojian Pharmaceutical's clinical trial application for its SSGJ-627 antibody injection, according to a Friday filing with the Shanghai Stock Exchange.

The injection is a recombinant anti-TL1A humanized monoclonal antibody, helping treat autoimmune and inflammatory conditions, the filing said.

data.title: TSMC's US shares rise on Q4 revenue beat
data.description:
** U.S.-listed shares of Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co shares up 1.3% to $209.73 premarket after co reports Q4 rev on AI demand

** Revenue in the October-December period of 2024 came in at T$868.42 bln ($26.36 bln), according to Reuters calculations, compared with an LSEG SmartEstimate of T$853.57 bln

** For December alone, TSMC's rev jumps 57.8% y-o-y to T$278.16 bln

** Co will report full Q4 earnings on Jan. 16, when it will update its outlook for the current quarter and full year

** Taiwan expects only a small impact from tariffs imposed by U.S. President-elect Donald Trump's administration on semiconductor exports given their technological superiority, Economy Minister Kuo Jyh-huei says

** 31 brokerages rate the stock "buy" or higher, 1 "hold"; their median PT is $1,380 - LSEG

($1 = 32.8850 Taiwan dollars)

data.title: Palm surges more than 2%, logs weekly gain
data.description:
Malaysian palm oil futures ended more than 2% higher on Friday, bouncing back to log a weekly gain, tracking stronger Chicago soyoil prices.

The benchmark palm oil contract for March delivery on the Bursa Malaysia Derivatives Exchange rose 97 ringgit, or 2.26%, to 4,393 ringgit ($977.74) a metric ton at the close.

The contract gained 0.57% this week.

"Crude palm oil futures were seen trading higher on the back of a strong recovery in overnight Chicago soyoil and during Asian hours today," said Anilkumar Bagani, head of research at Mumbai-based vegetable oil broker Sunvin Group.

Bagani, however, said the expectations of a drop in Indonesian palm oil reference price for February, uncertainty over the success of Indonesia's B40 biodiesel mandate and lacklusture Dalian palm olein futures capped the gains.

Dalian's most-active soyoil contract (DBYcv1) rose 0.88%, while its palm oil contract gained 0.21%. Soyoil prices on the Chicago Board of Trade were up 3.23%.

Palm oil tracks the price movements of rival edible oils, as it competes for a share of the global vegetable oils market.

Malaysia's palm oil stocks declined for a third consecutive month, falling 6.91% to 1.71 million metric tons at the end of December, while crude palm oil production fell 8.3% and exports plunged 9.97%, data from the Malaysian Palm Oil Board showed on Friday.

Cargo surveyors estimated that Malaysian palm oil exports fell between 21.4% and 26.8% during the Jan. 1-10 period.

Oil prices rose and were on track for a third straight week of gains as traders focused on potential supply disruptions from sanctions, while icy conditions in parts of the United States and Europe are expected to drive up fuel demand. O/R

Stronger crude oil futures make palm a more attractive option for biodiesel feedstock.

The Malaysian ringgit , palm's currency of trade, strengthened 0.16% against the dollar, making the commodity more expensive for buyers holding foreign currencies.

($1 = 4.4930 ringgit)

data.title: Tesla recalls about 239,000 vehicles over rear-view camera issue
data.description: Tesla said on Friday it was recalling about 239,000 vehicles over an issue that could cause the rear-view camera to not display an image, increasing the risk of a crash.

data.title: Update: KKR Extends Deadline for Tender Offer in Fuji Soft Take-Private Proposal
data.description:
(Updates with response from Fuji Soft in the last paragraph.)

KKR said Thursday it has extended its second tender offer to take Japan's Fuji Soft private until Jan. 24.

The 9,451 Japanese yen ($59.78) per share offer for shares of the software company it does not already own was scheduled to expire Thursday.

KKR said the extension is meant to "allow the company's shareholders and share option holders to make a considered decision" on the proposal.

The company also continued to disparage rival Bain Capital's offer to buy about 31.4 million shares or 49.9% of the company as a "hostile partial tender offer with the aim of seizing control of the company."

"If Bain Capital, with no outstanding conditions precedent to fulfil, has not launched an unsolicited tender offer as it said it would, it is because Bain Capital either has no actual intention to launch an unsolicited tender offer or that it is unable to do so," the company said.

KKR added that Bain Capital and allied members of Fuji Soft's founding family reached out through an intermediary in late December to propose working together to take the company private.

Bain Capital said it had no comment on the matter for now. Fuji Soft referred MT Newswires to KKR's amended tender offer.

data.title: Christmas Boosts Sainsbury's Sales as Grocery Market Share Grows
data.description:
J Sainsbury celebrated its "biggest ever Christmas" amid grocery market share gains and volume performance ahead of the industry, according to a business update of Friday.

For the 16 weeks ending Jan. 4, total retail sales and like-for-like sales, excluding fuel, rose by 2.7% and 2.8% year over year, respectively. Sainsbury's also gained grocery market share for the fifth consecutive Christmas, with holiday sales increasing 3.8% at Sainsbury's and 1.1% at Argos.

"Driven by our leading combination of quality, value and service, we have achieved seven consecutive quarters of volume performance ahead of the market and further accelerated our two-year volume growth," Chief Executive Simon Roberts commented.

In light of its strong fiscal third-quarter results, the retailer announced a 5% pay raise for hourly-paid staff in its retail division. Sainsbury's and Argos employees will see their hourly wages increase to 12.45 pounds in March and 12.60 pounds in August, while London-based staff will earn 13.70 pounds and 13.85 pounds during the same periods.

On the business front, the company expects retail underlying operating profit for fiscal 2025 to be at the midpoint of its guidance range of 1.01 billion pounds sterling to 1.06 billion pounds, aligning with market consensus. Total financial services underlying operating profit is expected to be 30 million pounds, above the previous guidance range of 15 million pounds to 25 million pounds.

RBC Capital Markets sees Sainsbury's as a "strong player" in the UK's food retailing sector, highlighting its progress in improving price-value credentials. "SBRY has a strong track record of cost efficiency, and we expect further cost savings to come near-term. Its balance sheet remains robust and the business is cash generative, which we expect to support a continuation of additional cash returns," analysts wrote.

Shares were down almost 3% Friday morning.

data.title: India rupee hits lifetime low, falls for 10th straight week
data.description:
The Indian rupee slipped to its all-time low on Friday as the dollar stood firm ahead of closely watched U.S. labour market data, keeping regional currencies on the defensive.

The rupee weakened to 85.97 against the U.S. dollar, inching past its previous record low of 85.9325 hit on Thursday. The currency ended at 85.9650, down 0.2% on the week and logged its tenth consecutive weekly loss.

The local unit has faced persistent headwinds over recent weeks on the back of a surging dollar and weak capital flows. However, routine interventions by the Reserve Bank of India have helped limit losses.

State-run banks were spotted offering dollars on Friday as well, most likely on behalf of the RBI, three traders told Reuters.

The rupee's fall below the psychologically important 86 level is likely "sooner rather than later," a trader at a foreign bank said.

The dollar index held north of the 109-handle, hovering close to a two-year peak ahead of U.S. non-farm payrolls data. The data will influence expectations of the pace and depth of rate cuts by the Federal Reserve this year.

"We think the balance of risks is tilted to the upside for the dollar today, as robust jobs figures could prompt markets to price out a March cut and potentially push the first fully-priced move beyond June," ING Bank said in a note.

Expectations surrounding the incoming U.S. President's policies and slower rate cuts by the Fed have boosted the dollar and U.S. bond yields over recent weeks. Meanwhile, foreign investors have pulled out over $3 billion from Indian stocks and bonds so far in January.

Both factors have kept the rupee under pressure with gauges that measure the currency's volatility also ticking higher in the one month since the change of guard at the Reserve Bank of India.

data.title: Brent Crude Oil Climbs to 3-Month High
data.description:
Brent crude oil futures rose over 2% to surpass $78.5 per barrel on Friday, reaching the highest level since October, driven by a drop in US crude stockpiles.

Cold weather in the US reduced inventory levels at Cushing, Oklahoma, to their lowest since 2014.

The global oil market is tightening due to reduced supply from key exporters like Russia and Iran, as well as a surge in demand for heating fuels.

The rise in oil prices is also linked to fears surrounding US policy under President-elect Donald Trump, including potential sanctions on Iran and trade conflicts that could disrupt energy flows.

Trump’s plans to authorize new drilling and impose tariffs on Canadian imports, including oil, further heightened market concerns.

Oil is set for a third straight weekly gain.

data.title: Shoals Technologies Group Files Patent Infringement Complaint Against Voltage
data.description:
Shoals Technologies Group said late Thursday it filed a new patent infringement complaint against Voltage with the US International Trade Commission.

The company said the complaint involves two new patents related to its big lead assembly offerings for solar panel arrays, seeking to block the importation of trunk bus lead assemblies that infringe its intellectual property.

Shoals believes the new patents "provide protection against alternative designs that Voltage may create to bypass" the original patent.

Voltage did not immediately respond to MT Newswires' request for comment.

data.title: Sterling Could Stay Weaker on Growth Concerns — Market Talk
data.description: Sterling could face further weakness in early 2025 following the recent jump in yields of U.K. government bonds, or gilts, Swissquote Bank's Ipek Ozkardeskaya says in a note. The selloff in gilts and sterling has cooled but the cost of boosting economic growth has become "significantly more expensive for the U.K. government," she says. That means the U.K. economy may not perform as well as it did last year. "And that sets the pound outlook negative at the early weeks of the new year." (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1020 GMT - Sterling could face further weakness in early 2025 following the recent jump in yields of U.K. government bonds, or gilts, Swissquote Bank's Ipek Ozkardeskaya says in a note. The selloff in gilts and sterling has cooled but the cost of boosting economic growth has become "significantly more expensive for the U.K. government," she says. That means the U.K. economy may not perform as well as it did last year. "And that sets the pound outlook negative at the early weeks of the new year." (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0923 GMT - Sterling could remain under pressure in the near-term on concerns about higher U.K. borrowing costs but is likely to recover later in the year, UBS Global Wealth Management analysts say in a note. "A likely more cautious rate-cutting stance from the Bank of England versus its peers--as well as an eventual softening of the dollar's overvaluation--still supports a sterling revival to $1.34 by year-end," they say. With volatility and a weaker sterling set to persist in the near-term, investors could find value in structured strategies that generate income or gradually accumulate sterling until fiscal policy uncertainty eases and investor confidence rebounds. Sterling falls 0.1% to $1.2291. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0918 GMT - U.K. government-bond yields rise in early trade, although they stay below multi-year highs reached Thursday. Investors will be watching to see whether gilt-market turbulence resumes and Friday's key U.S. jobs data at 1330 GMT have potential to cause volatility, says Tickmill Group's Patrick Munnelly. The key question would be whether the Bank of England opts to intervene to stabilize gilts if it sees signs of "disorderly markets," he says. The 30-year gilt yield rises 3 basis points to 5.392%, having hit its highest since 1998 at 5.474% early Thursday, LSEG data show. The 10-year yield rises 3 basis points to 4.831%, having hit 4.921% Thursday, the highest level since 2008. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

0918 GMT - The dollar is unlikely to move materially unless the U.S. nonfarm payrolls report later is much stronger or weaker than expected, MUFG Bank's Derek Halpenny says in a note. Given the clear rhetoric from Federal Reserve officials about slowing interest rate cuts and with Donald Trump's January 20 presidential inauguration approaching, it would likely take a notable miss versus expectations to see the dollar move significantly, he says. "We remain dollar bullish but moves could be more contained until we get confirmation of trade tariff announcements on or soon after inauguration day." The payrolls data will be released at 1330 GMT. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0910 GMT - Gold is headed for a weekly gain of roughly 2% as uncertainty around future U.S. policy under President-elect Donald Trump boosts safe-haven demand. In early trade, futures are up 0.6% to $2,706.70 a troy ounce, with traders awaiting a key U.S. jobs report later on Friday for more cues on the path of interest rates. According to Swissquote, activity on Fed funds futures suggests the next cut would be in May. But "a set of stronger-than-expected [jobs] data could flip this expectation to the 'no cut until June' side very rapidly, enhance the selloff in the U.S. treasurys and support a further appreciation of the U.S. dollar," senior analyst Ipek Ozkardeskaya says in a note to clients. The yellow metal is seen as a hedge against inflation, but higher interest rates reduce the appeal of holding the non-yielding asset. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0834 GMT - Singapore's economic growth will likely remain resilient and above potential this year, Nomura economists write in a note. Singapore's free-trade agreement with the U.S., plus strong local wage and employment growth, will likely help cushion against shocks from rising trade protectionism, they say. Nomura maintains its 2025 GDP growth forecast at 2.8%, near the top end of the official 1.0%-3.0% forecast range. This reflects the brokerage's view that electronics exports will stay supportive of manufacturing output growth in 1H. However, the forecast also represents a moderation from growth of 4.0% last year, partly due to expected weaker external demand under Trump 2.0, they say.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0821 GMT - Sterling trims losses sustained Thursday following a sharp rise in U.K. government bond yields as a U.K. Treasury spokesperson said meeting the fiscal rules is non-negotiable. That means Treasury chief Rachel Reeves could deliver some fiscal consolidation if the updated Office for Budget Responsibility forecasts in March show the government isn't on track to meet fiscal rules, ING's Francesco Pesole says in a note. "The market seems to be acknowledging the Treasury's reiterated fiscal pledges and this has prevented the gilt and pound selloffs from becoming disorderly." GBP/USD falls 0.2% to 1.2288 after hitting a 14-month low of 1.2239 Thursday, according to FactSet. EUR/GBP rises 0.1% to 0.8280 after reaching a two-month high of 0.8407 Thursday.(renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0813 GMT - The continued cooling of Singapore's underlying inflation supports Nomura's view that the central bank could ease monetary policy settings later this month. The brokerage's FX strategy team. now assigns a 55% likelihood to the MAS easing its policy via a slight reduction in the slope of S$NEER appreciation, up from 40% previously. The MAS likely views current policy settings as restrictive given the risk posed by Trump's tariffs, say Euben Paracuelles and Charnon Boonnuch in a note. Nomura continues to expect 2025 average core inflation at 1.6% on year, near the lower end of MAS's forecast range of 1.5%-3.0%. However, it sees downside risks after a broad-based, sharper-than-expected decline in November from October. MAS is due to announce its policy decision no later than Jan. 31. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0809 GMT - Bank Indonesia's strong emphasis on foreign-exchange stability will likely make it difficult to deliver additional rate cuts in the coming months, Nomura analysts say. That's particularly true given that President-elect Trump is threatening to raise tariffs on imports and the Fed is turning less dovish, Euben Paracuelles and Nabila Amani say in a note. The economists pencil in one 25bp cut in 4Q 2025. The Indonesian central bank meets next week. They see Indonesia's GDP growth slowing slightly from an estimated 5.0% expansion in 2024. Household spending will likely be held back by still-weak consumer sentiment in 1H before edging up in 2H as public-sector spending improves. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

0758 GMT - The dollar rises ahead of U.S. nonfarm payrolls data that could provide hints on the timing of the Federal Reserve's next interest-rate cut. The balance of risks are tilted towards dollar gains as possibly robust jobs figures could "prompt markets to price out a March rate cut and potentially push the first fully-priced move beyond June," ING's Francesco Pesole says in a note. In the event of weaker-than-expected data, investors could trim dollar long positions that bet on its rise. However, these longs could be rebuilt at better levels ahead of key upcoming data and Donald Trump's January 20 presidential inauguration. The jobs data are due at 1330 GMT. The DXY dollar index rises 0.1% to 109.272.(renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0755 GMT - U.K. government bonds, or gilts, have fared worse than others within a recent global government bond selloff, but they could recover soon, Capital Economics's Hubert de Barochez says in a note. "We think that bonds will recover before long, with yields in the U.K. falling particularly sharply by the end of this year," the senior markets economist says in a note. Gilts started to perform weakly in mid-September and the selloff has been particularly acute at the long end of the curve, especially during a second phase since early December, he says. "To some extent, this has reflected a broader trend." Government bonds have sold off across the developed world, led by the U.S, on growing concerns about Trump's policies. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0747 GMT - The Monetary Authority of Singapore is in no rush to ease monetary policy settings, CIMB analysts say, reiterating their call for a hold until April. Although inflation appears to be easing, the central bank will likely err on the side of caution, they write in a note. MAS may want to wait to assess the impact of U.S. President-elect Trump's policies first, they say. Significant trade, growth and inflation uncertainty stemming from Trump's policies could lead to some market volatility after his inauguration later this month, they say. Other events to look out for include Singapore's budget in February and geopolitical risks in Eastern Europe, the analysts add.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

data.title: AkzoNobel to Streamline Operations in France, Sees 182 Job Cuts
data.description:
By Ian Walker

AkzoNobel plans to streamline its operations in France with the result of 182 net jobs being cut.

The Dutch paints company--which houses the Dulux, Polycell and Cuprinol brands--said Friday that it will invest 22 million euros ($22.7 million) in a paint production site in Montataire as part of its plan to streamline operations and drive growth.

As part of the plan Akzo will reorganize its commercial and support function and distribution network, including transferring some outlets to independent partners and some closures.

The changes will lead to 211 job cuts, although 29 new jobs are expected to be created, the company said. Akzo employs 1,300 people in France.

The changes will start in May and continue through to the end of next year.

Write to Ian Walker at ian.walker@wsj.com

data.title: IAR resumes share repurchase program
data.description:
Uppsala, Sweden; 10 January 2025 — The Board of Directors of I.A.R. Systems Group AB ("IAR") has decided to resume the share repurchase program of its own Class B shares, initially announced on 3 September 2024 and paused on 13 December 2024. All acquisitions will be carried out on Nasdaq Stockholm by Danske Bank on behalf of IAR, starting 10 January 2025 and continuing until 27 April 2025, unless otherwise communicated. The purpose of the repurchase program is to provide the Board with increased flexibility in managing the company's capital structure.

The company's total holding of its own shares amounts to 1,003,011, of which 694,969 are Class B shares and 308,042 are Class C shares, corresponding to 7.18 percent of all shares and 5.09 percent of all votes in the company. The total number of shares in IAR, including treasury shares, amounts to 13,968,333, while the number of outstanding shares, excluding treasury shares, amounts to 12,965,322.

data.title: New Gold Reports Increase in Q4 Gold, Copper Production
data.description:
New Gold reported Q4 gold production late Thursday of 80,438 ounces and copper production of 14.5 million pounds, reflecting a 2% and 20% increase, respectively, over the previous year.

"The fourth quarter was our highest production quarter of the year," said CEO Patrick Godin. "This was during a quarter that saw New Gold deliver on key growth milestones including commercial production at New Afton's C-Zone and first ore from Rainy River underground, both ahead of schedule."

For 2024, the company said consolidated gold production reached 298,303 ounces, slightly below the updated guidance range of 300,000 to 310,000 ounces.

Full-year copper production hit 54 million pounds, meeting the midpoint of initial guidance, the company said.

data.title: FX stable and stocks decline ahead of US labour data
data.description:
Key points:
  • MSCI FX index set for first weekly gain in six
  • Turkish unemployment falls, industrial output gains in November
  • Ethiopia to launch securities exchange on day
  • MSCI FX flat, stocks down 0.7%

Emerging market stock markets hit their lowest in nearly four months, while currencies traded around flat on Friday as investors awaited a U.S. labour market report for insight into future interest rate cuts in the world's largest economy.

The MSCI index of EM equities CBOE:EFS fell 0.7%, the lowest since September 2024. On Thursday it had ended more than 10% below its 2024 peak after hitting a two-year high in October.

The MSCI FX index (.MIEM00000CUS) was little changed, but was set for its first weekly gain in six.

The Turkish lira set a record low against the dollar, falling 0.3% to 35.4500. Data showed the unemployment rate dropped to 8.6% in November and industrial production rose by 2.9% month on month.

The South African rand weakened against the dollar, on track for its fourth straight session of declines, while the Kenyan shilling inched 0.2% higher.

Market participants are focused on the nonfarm payrolls (NFP) data out of the U.S. at 1330 GMT, for clues on the Federal Reserve's monetary policy path after the central bank struck a hawkish tone in December.

"As long as the NFP are not significantly weaker-than-expected, market concerns that U.S. Treasury yields may rise even further – driven by the prospects of expansionary fiscal policies of President-elect Trump accompanied by trade tariffs – may prevail, leaving investors generally cautious towards EM (investments)," said Piotr Matys, senior FX analyst at In Touch Capital Markets.

Reports on the extent of Trump's threatened tariffs that fostered expectations they would be less aggressive than previously thought initially boosted EM markets this week.

However, conflicting reports on tariffs, compounded by strong U.S. labour market data that raised expectations for fewer Fed rate cuts, led the main EM indexes to surrender gains as U.S. yields and the dollar rose.

Among stocks, Romania's main stock index was among the biggest gainers, with a 0.4% rise. Data showed the country's economy grew 1.2% in the third quarter.

Meanwhile, a draft bill said Romania's coalition government would raise the ceiling of its foreign debt issuance framework on Friday to enable it to issue an estimated 13 billion euros ($13.37 billion) of Eurobonds in 2025.

Most emerging European currencies were steady against the euro , with the Polish zloty rising about 0.2%.

Ethiopia is launching a securities exchange, a milestone in Prime Minister Abiy Ahmed's drive to open up the country's tightly controlled economy to private investment.

One of the first companies expected to be listed is state-owned telecoms company Ethio Telecom, where a 10% stake is earmarked for to be floated.

HIGHLIGHTS:

** China central bank is moving faster towards its policy limits, suspends treasury bond purchases

** Euro zone government bond yields hit multi-month highs

For TOP NEWS across emerging markets
